The School-Treat

Gerda had refused point-blank to invite Darnley and Christie to supper on Monday night, thus bringing to nothing ChristieвАЩs premonition in the stubble-field. And now it was the middle of the long, sunlit afternoonвБ†вАФrelaxed, autumnal, mellowвБ†вАФof Mr.¬†ValleyвАЩs great gala-day.

The f√™te was held in the vicarage-glebe, adjoining that portion of the churchyard-wall behind which rose the now four-months-old tombstone of the youthful Mr.¬†Redfern.

The young men and boys of the village, encouraged at their game by Mr.¬†Urquhart and Darnley Otter, were engaged in an interminable cricket-match, a match played between those who lived west of the church and those who lived east.

When Wolf first left his employerвАЩs library, which he did some half-hour later than the squire himself, and entered the school-treat field, he felt nervous and irritable. Everyone he knew in the world seemed to be gathered in that enclosure; and as he stealthily shuffled along the edge of the churchyard, he felt as if he would like to hide himself from them all, down in the silent earth along with young Redfern!

He found himself at a spot where the wall was very low, and, turning his back upon the crowded scene, leaned there for a while unnoticed, gazing at the great perpendicular tower. With the shouts and laughter in his ears, that tower looked incredibly massive and silent. What ebbing and flowing of human lives had it not seen, since unknown hands in the reign of the first Tudor piled it up there, stone upon stone!

Well, at least it was something to face the disquietudes of his own life in the presence of masonry like this, so subdued, so encrusted, rendered so mellow by the passing of the generations! As long as Fate allowed him to eke out his days amid old time-weathered concretions, like this KingвАЩs Barton Tower, he could never touch certain abysses of misery! Here in these West Country places he was at any rate spared the atrocity of feeling the pinch of lifeвАЩs dilemmas against a background of monstrous modern inventions. The long, cold clutch of scientific discovery, laid, like metallic fingers, upon the human pulse, could not despoil the dignity of existence here; though the invasion by such inhuman forces had already begun!

вАЬLong may this tower stand, so that men like me can touch its stones, its buttresses, its lichen, its moss, and escape from the dragonвАЩs-tail of the stinging present!вАЭ

He was conscious of a stealthy step behind him, and, turning round, he found Jason Otter at his side.

вАЬYou are enjoying yourself looking at his grave,вАЭ the poet began; вАЬand I donвАЩt blame you. I like looking at the graves of people IвАЩve known. But you go further than I could go, Solent. You are the clever one, the wise one, the old cunning one! You can enjoy looking at a grave though you never knew the person whoвАЩs in it.вАЭ

вАЬYou canвАЩt expect me not to be interested in Redfern, can you?вАЭ retorted Wolf, a little crustily.

вАЬOf course not. ThatвАЩs just it. We all feel an interestвБ†вАФa nice, merry interestвБ†вАФin being alive when someone else is dead. He only came down here for money,вАЭ he added unexpectedly, вАЬlike you!вАЭ

вАЬIf I came for it, I assure you I donвАЩt get it,вАЭ said Wolf.

Jason chuckled a great deal at this remark. Then he grew grave. вАЬIвАЩve got a poem here IвАЩd like to read to you, if it wouldnвАЩt spoil your pleasure in looking at this young manвАЩs grave. I wonвАЩt, if it would.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩve looked all I want to look,вАЭ said Wolf; вАЬso do read me what youвАЩve got there. IвАЩm glad of any excuse not to go round the field and hear so much talk.вАЭ

вАЬSit down, then, a minuteвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ do you mind?вАЭ

The two men sat down at the base of the wall and leaned their backs against it, facing the school-treat meadow. Jason produced from his pocket a small notebook, which he opened very deliberately upon his knee.

вАЬItвАЩs about white seaweed,вАЭ said Jason Otter.

вАЬI didnвАЩt know it was ever white,вАЭ said Wolf.

вАЬEverything is white at one time or another,вАЭ retorted Jason. вАЬYouвАЩll be white enough yourself, one fine day!вАЭ

вАЬIf it only gets white when itвАЩs dead,вАЭ argued Wolf obstinately, вАЬI donвАЩt think itвАЩs much of a subject. I like the idea of seaweed being white in the way chalk is white or daisies are white; but if it just fades and bleachesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I donвАЩt think much of that.вАЭ

вАЬItвАЩs no good abusing me before youвАЩve heard it,вАЭ said Jason; вАЬbut, of course, we know this business of reading our writings is what your friend Darnley would call impolite.вАЭ

вАЬGo on, man, go on!вАЭ cried Wolf. вАЬIвАЩm listening.вАЭ

And the poet began to read.

вАЬWhite SeaweedвАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

He repeated these words a second time, gathering his energy.

вАЬWhite Seaweed.вАЭ

вАЬFor GodвАЩs sake,вАЭ cried Wolf, вАЬget on with it! TheyвАЩll catch sight of us in a moment and then itвАЩll all be spoilt.вАЭ

Jason accepted this impatience with unruffled equanimity, and began in a low voice; but, gathering confidence as he proceeded, he read the poem from beginning to end without a pause.

White as the foam in the track of a whale
As he spouts and sports for a thousand miles
Where the waters slope round the planetвАЩs rim,
Beyond the continents and the isles,
White as the foam that follows him
Where thereвАЩs never a masthead nor a sail.

Drowned and dead from their sunken ships,
Drift the bodies of boys and girls;
White are they as they float and drift,
Their hair like flotsam, their breasts like pearls,
While the grey tides lift them, or cease to lift,
And the green tides gurgle between their lips.

FishesвАЩ eyes in the cold grey deep,
Staring and waiting, waiting and staring,
SeagullsвАЩ beaks on the tops of the wave,
The same eternal quest are sharing;
But the dark, wet, purple, slippery grave
Holds safe those bodies in untouched sleep.

And out of the flesh of those bodies light,
In their dark, wet, purple, slippery bed,
A seaweed grows that is soft as silk,
White as the moon on St.¬†AlbanвАЩs Head,
Moss-like, fern-like, white as milk.вБ†вАФ
The fingers of Mary are not more white!

Oh! White as the horn of GodвАЩs unicorn,
That seaweed lies upon Redcliff bay,
Lies in the spindrift on Redcliff sands,
Fling all your wicked thoughts away!
Take off your shoes; anoint your hands!
Than to touch such seaweed with careless scorn
вАЩTwere better never to have been born!

JasonвАЩs voice sank; and that peculiar silence ensued which is fuller of electric crosscurrents than anything else in the worldвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ the silence produced by the falling of the seminal drops of verbal creationвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ upon an alien mind.

вАЬI like it very much,вАЭ murmured Wolf at last. And he thought to himself, вАЬThe beggar has his own peculiar imagination.вАЭ

Then he said aloud: вАЬItвАЩs one of your best poems, Jason. I donвАЩt think itвАЩs quite up to the вАШSlowworm of Lenty,вАЩ but it does you credit and I congratulate you. What did you exactly mean by that last verse? Did you mean that there are people in the world whose wicked thoughts are aroused by white seaweed, or did you just mean the ordinary stupidity of human beings?вАЭ

вАЬItвАЩs not my business to explain what I mean,вАЭ said Jason. вАЬItвАЩs my business to write. I can see what you think. You think that I just string words together as they come into my head! It isnвАЩt as easy to write a poem as you seem to imagine.вАЭ

вАЬWhy do you write so often about water and about drowned people?вАЭ asked Wolf. вАЬYour pond-elf in вАШThe SlowwormвАЩ gave me a weird feeling; and this seaweed of yours, growing out of drowned bodiesвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬYou neednвАЩt go on!вАЭ interrupted Jason. вАЬOf course, I canвАЩt expect anyone to like my poetry who lives by copying out the liquorish thoughts of a doting old fool. We all want to be glorified. My poetry is all IвАЩve got and I ought never to have read it to you. I ought to have known IвАЩd only get abuse. ItвАЩs this wanting to be glorified thatвАЩs the mistake. A person ought to be satisfied if he can get his meals three times a day, without having to dance attendance on some silly old man or some ugly old woman!вАЭ

Wolf swept this aside. вАЬDo you have in your mind any definite people when you make the newts and tadpoles tease the pond-elf, and when you make these fish and gulls want to eat these youthful bodies?вАЭ

JasonвАЩs face wrinkled with delight at this.

вАЬYouвАЩre afraid I might bring you in!вАЭ he chuckled. вАЬI wouldnвАЩt mind not being glorified if I could make your friend Urquhart agitate himself as much as you do over my poems.вАЭ

Wolf had no time to reply to this; for, to his considerable surprise, he perceived his mother and Gerda, arm in arm, advancing towards their retreat.

Both he and Jason struggled simultaneously to their feet and moved towards the two women. Mrs.¬†Solent began speaking with her accustomed high-pitched ironical intonation.

вАЬDonвАЩt take it off, Mr.¬†Otter,вАЭ she said, when the poet raised his hand to his hat. вАЬI know how you hate the sun; and it is hot today; though the hotter it is the more I enjoy it, though I think our pretty Gerda here agrees with you.вАЭ

Jason, who had succeeded with a certain embarrassment in lifting his straw-hat a few inches from his head in a stiff, perpendicular direction, pulled it down once more over his forehead with grateful relief.

вАЬWhatвАЩs this?вАЭ said Wolf, trying to conceal his discomfort under an airy jauntiness. вАЬWhatвАЩs this between you two?вАЭ

вАЬYour mother and I have had several walks together,вАЭ said Jason, вАЬand she knows my ways.вАЭ

вАЬSo well as to take a great liberty!вАЭ exclaimed the handsome lady, whose brown eyes were shining with radiant exultation. And as she spoke she stepped to JasonвАЩs side and poked something, with her light-gloved fingers, into its place under his hat.

While this was proceeding, the expression upon the poetвАЩs face made Wolf astonished. It was the queerest mixture of physical repulsion with pleasurable, masochistic submission. He was amazed at his motherвАЩs audacity.

вАЬWhat is it that you wear under your hat, Mr.¬†Otter?вАЭ asked Gerda innocently.

The strange man looked at her with a very peculiar expressionвБ†вАФan expression that baffled Wolf altogether. Then a most beautiful look came into his grey eyes, a look infinitely wistful and sorrowful, the sort of look that a disguised and persecuted god, lost among some savage race that knew him not nor could have comprehended him if it had known him, might have worn; and he replied gently: вАЬI feel the sun, young lady. I find cabbage-leaves a great help. But todayвАЭвБ†вАФand here he smiled a disarming smileвБ†вАФвАЬtoday itвАЩs a rhubarb-leaf.вАЭ

Having said this, and with a courtly bend of his body that would have done credit to a royal personage, Jason Otter moved off, making his way, with careful manoeuvring to avoid any encounter with the crowd, towards that part of the field where the old men of the village, seated on wooden benches, were partaking of cakes and cider.

вАЬI hope you havenвАЩt offended him, Mother,вАЭ muttered Wolf.

вАЬI donвАЩt think so,вАЭ cried Gerda. вАЬWhat a nice man he is, Wolf! I like him ever so much better than Darnley.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs because DarnleyвАЩs my best friend,вАЭ said Wolf. вАЬItвАЩs a law of nature, sweetheart, isnвАЩt it, Mother?вАЭ

But Mrs.¬†Solent completely disregarded this little passage between them.

вАЬWhat Gerda and I came for,вАЭ she said, вАЬwas to ask you to show us Mr.¬†RedfernвАЩs grave. GerdaвАЩs never seen it, though her father made the headstone, and IвАЩve never seen it, though IвАЩve asked Mr.¬†Urquhart a hundred times to show it to me.вАЭ

вАЬItвАЩs not hard to find,вАЭ said Wolf drily. вАЬYou could have gone any day by yourself.вАЭ

вАЬWhatвАЩs the sport in that?вАЭ laughed the lady, still displaying the same undercurrent of secret excitement. вАЬThe fun of looking at graves is all in the person you look at them withвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ isnвАЩt it, Gerda? IвАЩm sure you must have enjoyed yourself watching all the fuss people make!вАЭ

вАЬI canвАЩt help my father being a monument-maker,вАЭ said Gerda gravely. вАЬItвАЩs a trade, like any other trade.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm not quarrelling with your fatherвАЩs profession, child,вАЭ Mrs.¬†Solent rapped out. вАЬIвАЩm only saying that thereвАЩs no sport in looking at graves by oneself; and I do want to see this one.вАЭ

вАЬThere it is, then, Mother!вАЭ cried Wolf, almost peevishly. вАЬCanвАЩt you see?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ the tall stone one thereвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ no! over thereвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ nearer the Tower,вАЭ and he pointed with his stick.

вАЬI want to go up to it,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Solent obstinately, вАЬand so does Gerda. She told me so just now. WeвАЩre both sick to death of swinging long-legged girls. I donвАЩt want to see any more frills or garters for the rest of my life.вАЭ

вАЬWell, come on, then,вАЭ said Wolf petulantly. вАЬYou can climb over this, canвАЩt you, Mother? I suppose Bob WeevilвАЩs making himself useful at the swings, eh?вАЭ

Whatever demon it was that made him indulge in this jocularity, its result was immediate.

Gerda turned on him fiercely. вАЬDonвАЩt be so vulgar, Wolf. BobвАЩs playing cricket, and soвАЩs Lobbie. You ought to know better than to make remarks like that!вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt push me, Wolf.вАЭ It was his mother speaking, as she began scrambling over the low moss-grown wall. вАЬGive me your hand;вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ no! give me your hand.вАЭ

Soon they were all three standing by RedfernвАЩs grave.

вАЬPoor boy!вАЭ sighed Mrs.¬†Solent. вАЬDo you know, Wolf, I heard Roger Monk talking in a queer way last week. I was asking him about this boyвАЩs death, and he spoke in such a funny tone about it. He almost implied that it was a case of suicide. Have you heard anything of that sort?вАЭ

вАЬOh, just rumours, Mother,вАЭ replied Wolf casually; вАЬjust rumours and village-gossip. IвАЩve never heard of an inquest, or anything like that. I believe he died in his bed.вАЭ

вАЬFather talks queer about it too,вАЭ said Gerda. вАЬBut do look at that! Is that a mole or a rabbit?вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt know,вАЭ said Wolf vaguely. It did not interest him in any particular way that this newly-grown-over mound should have been burrowed into or scraped at. After his many years of London life, the ways of moles, rabbits, dogs, foxes, were all equally arbitrary, equally unpredictable. It was, however, brought home to him now that there was something exceptional in this phenomenon; for Gerda, oblivious of the risk of grass-stains upon her summer frock, went down hurriedly on her knees and began fumbling with her bare fingers in the disordered clay, scooping up little handsfuls of dry brown earth with one hand and filtering them thoughtfully into the hollow palm of the other hand.

вАЬAre you looking for Mr.¬†RedfernвАЩs bones?вАЭ enquired Mrs.¬†Solent in her most airy manner. вАЬYou look like that pretty girl in the poem, leaning over her Pot of Basil; doesnвАЩt she, Wolf?вАЭ And touching the mound with the tip of her green parasol she put her head a little to one side and began quoting from the poem in question in a mock-sentimental intonationвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬAnd she forgot the stars, the moon, the sun;
And she forgot the blue above the trees;
And she forgot the dells where waters run;
And she forgot the chilly autumn breeze.
She had no knowledgeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt, Mother,вАЭ interrupted Wolf crossly, вАЬGerda knows what sheвАЩs doing.вАЭ

The unequalled lines roused their response in him, as independently of the mocking tone in which they were spoken as beautiful limbs under a ridiculous disguise; but this response only annoyed him the more.

вАЬWhat is it, sweetheart?вАЭ he cried. вАЬIs it a rabbit? I didnвАЩt know rabbits ever burrowed in churchyards.вАЭ

вАЬItвАЩs a mole,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Solent.

It was WolfвАЩs turn to mutter something nowвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬWell said, old mole! canst work iвАЩ the earth so fast?
A worthy pioneer! Once more remove, good friends.вАЭ

вАЬWhatвАЩs that? You know perfectly well itвАЩs a mole, Gerda,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Solent. Gerda remained silent. She lifted some of the loose earth to her face and smelt it. Then she leapt to her feet, shook out her skirt, and rubbed the palms of her hands together. вАЬI give it up,вАЭ she said. вАЬIt isnвАЩt a rabbit. ThereвАЩs no smell of a fox either. It may have been a dog.вАЭ

вАЬA moleвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ a mole!вАЭ repeated the older woman.

вАЬA mole!вАЭ muttered Gerda, with the profound sarcasm of the country-bred; and Wolf caught a little red flush on her cheeks like a crimson shadow on a mother-of-pearl shell. вАЬWell! we canвАЩt do anything, anyway,вАЭ she said. вАЬItвАЩs silly to fuss ourselves. Bother! IвАЩve got some grit in my shoe!вАЭ

вАЬIt spoils the look of the grave completely, this great mole-hole,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Solent. Then her face lit up, and she opened her parasol with an eager click. вАЬThis is a bit of sport,вАЭ she cried. вАЬLetвАЩs fill the thing up! Never mind about the school-treat. Where does Valley keep his spade? We only want a spade and a roll of turf. I saw some loose turf lying about in our garden. Come on, Wolf! LetвАЩs go over and get it, and ask Valley where he keeps his shovel.вАЭ

Her face was full of animation now, and her eyes shone. Her grey hair and black Gainsborough hat framed the vivid cheeks of youth. The way she tilted her parasol as she spoke had something adventurous, almost hoydenish.

вАЬCome, Wolf, letвАЩs get that turf,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Solent. вАЬWe must ask Valley where he keeps his spade.вАЭ

As Wolf turned to follow his mother on this impetuous quest, he caught sight of Gerda, struggling with the strap of her shoe, as she propped herself with one hand upon RedfernвАЩs headstone. There was such a look of defiant anger on her face that he halted irresolutely.

вАЬOh, go, if you want to, Wolf!вАЭ she cried. вАЬIвАЩm sure I donвАЩt want to keep you. It isnвАЩt often, though, that I get a chance of enjoying myself, working like I do in that dark kitchen all the time!вАЭ

Mrs.¬†Solent gave her a steady, surprised stare.

вАЬI wonвАЩt keep him long, if you want him for your game,вАЭ she said. вАЬI can fill this hole up by myself, if you just get me the spade and the turf, Wolf.вАЭ

The flush in GerdaвАЩs cheeks grew deeper. вАЬI think itвАЩs a shame! Why did you bring me here at all, Wolf, if we werenвАЩt going to do something nice? I donвАЩt want to spend this afternoon doing what I do every day in the week.вАЭ

Mrs.¬†Solent gave Wolf a quick, surprised look, full of airy pityвБ†вАФa look that said, вАЬYou poor boy, how awful for you to be at the beck and call of such a child!вАЭ But aloud she remarked:

вАЬItвАЩs all right, Gerda. We wonвАЩt spoil your sport. Run along to your friends. I wonвАЩt keep him long.вАЭ

But GerdaвАЩs suppressed anger had mounted so high by this time that there could be no such easy d√©nouement.

She held up her rounded chin and tossed back her head. Then, clasping her hands behind her, with her heels close together at the edge of the grave, she regarded Mrs.¬†Solent with flashing eyes.

вАЬOf course WolfвАЩs on your side. Of course heвАЩll love to fool about with your spades and turf, when itвАЩs my one real treat of the whole summer! You two are both the same. You only think of yourselves and what you want. If itвАЩs the silliest thing, like this nonsense about a mole, and every sensible person knows what a molehill is, it must come first, before everything, just because youвАЩve thought of it! Oh, yes, I saw you smiling at him just now, when my shoe came off. You couldnвАЩt have looked much different if my stockings had been full of holes! Everyone canвАЩt buy high-class London things; but I tell you our Blacksod shops be as good as they be any day in the week!вАЭ

вАЬWell, Wolf,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Solent calmly, holding her parasol at a correct garden-party angle and letting her high spirits drop away, вАЬthe best thing you can do is to take your pretty young wife back to her friendsвАЩ games.вАЭ

вАЬMy friendsвАЩ games!вАЭ retorted the indignant girl. вАЬIвАЩm as old as anyone, considering all I put up with!вАЭ

вАЬMy dear child,вАЭ said the elder lady gently. вАЬThereвАЩs really no reason for this excitement. Do try and calm yourself, and letвАЩs all go back quietly. IвАЩm sure IвАЩm quite ready to give up my idea if it spoils your pleasure. DonвАЩt, for mercyвАЩs sake, make such a mountain out of this molehill. I only thought of filling up this hole as a bit of sport, and because school-treats are so boring.вАЭ

Her words were soothing; but there was something in the tilt of her eyebrows, as she glanced at Wolf, which made him realize that she was less unruffled than she appeared. He knew of old that the one thing in the world she hated was any display of temper or anything resembling a вАЬscene.вАЭ His own mind at this moment was unable to resist its furtive commentary upon the way Chance had managed to stage this encounter between the two. He had noticed these tricks before. It was as if there were some special aesthetic laws which Chance delighted to obey; and it always gave him a peculiar satisfaction to contemplate this bizarre rhythm. At such moments he found himself sacrificing action, emotion, sympathy, every human attribute, in a sort of ecstatic pondering over what this artistry of Chance was accomplishing. He felt as if he were in the presence of the unrolling of a psychic map. The figures on this mapвБ†вАФhis mother with her green parasol, Gerda with her grass-stained dressвБ†вАФwere a sort of eddying vortex of significance upon a stream that was always rippling itself into mystic diagrams! Chance, in fact, was forever at work fulfilling its own secret aesthetic laws; but every now and then, as at this fatal moment, its creation became especially vivid, and the whole вАЬpsychic mapвАЭ upon that flowing stream grew violently and intensely agitated. The circle of ripples he was now contemplating with this inhuman detachment had two circumferences, namely, the angry consciousness of Gerda and the supercilious consciousness of his mother; but below them bothвБ†вАФdown there on the quiet river-floorвБ†вАФwas the discolored, decomposed, unrecognizable face of the young Redfern.

вАЬYouвАЩve never liked my marrying him!вАЭ It was GerdaвАЩs voice he heard now, as he awoke from his metaphysical trance to realize that part of his motherвАЩs last remarks had fallen upon nothing but the surface of his mind.

вАЬIвАЩve always been an outsider to both of you,вАЭ the angry girl went on. вАЬYouвАЩve always despised me and my family, and done your best to make him despise us.вАЭ

вАЬI have the greatest respect for your family, my good child. No one who knows your father can possibly help it. Come now! It really wonвАЩt do for us to make Wolf embarrassed like this. IвАЩve the utmost respect for your people, Gerda, and IвАЩm sure my son couldnвАЩt have married a lovelier creature than you are, even at this moment! But do come, now, both of you, and letвАЩs get back to the field. Mr.¬†Urquhart will be quite lost among those boys without WolfвАЩs help.вАЭ

She laid her hand with a soothing gesture upon the girlвАЩs wrist; but the glance she gave Wolf was full of a mocking resignation that threw a screen round them and railed off this ill-advised proletarian. GerdaвАЩs behaviour on the other side of this barrier became so irrational that it could only excite well-bred surprise! But the girl tossed her hand away.

вАЬMr.¬†Urquhart, indeed!вАЭ she cried. вАЬA nice sort you are out here, you KingвАЩs Barton gentry! Why, IвАЩve never cared even to tell Wolf all IвАЩve heard Dad say about what some folks do in this dirty village.вАЭ Her voice grew louder, as her long-suppressed feelings burst forth. Wolf had fancied in his simplicity that his motherвАЩs airy propitiations had disarmed the girl; but he underrated both GerdaвАЩs perspicacity and her pride.

There was something else on GerdaвАЩs mind, too, beyond her personal indignation. What actually, he wondered, were these Blacksod gossips saying? He looked at the girl with a kind of paralyzed helplessness, and again the thought struck him how neat a stroke of chance it was that RedfernвАЩs grave should be the background of her outburst.

вАЬSome of you gentry,вАЭ she went on fiercely, вАЬdonвАЩt lie abed with decent consciences like my folks! Why, they do say down at FarmerвАЩs Rest that landlord Round do keep his bed, and that Squire Urquhart can get no peace by night or by day, because of what do taunt their minds over this poor young man.вАЭ

In spite of his discomfort, Wolf couldnвАЩt help feeling faintly amused at GerdaвАЩs struggle to keep the insidious Dorset dialect out of her speech, a struggle that grew less and less availing as her agitation rose.

вАЬAnd these be the high-class people that you think so superior to respectable plain folk like my dear Dad!вАЭ Her voice had a quiver in it at this point that made Wolf cry out, вАЬGerda! Gerda darling!вАЭ But she did not break down. On the contrary, her tone grew stronger and more defiant. вАЬLike my dear Dad,вАЭ she went on, вАЬwho never in his whole life said an evil word to anyone. But you get your spade and your turf and cover up this hole. Maybe youвАЩll catch the fox that made it and be surprised!вАЭ

вАЬCome on, come on, Gerda,вАЭ said Wolf peevishly, stretching out his hand, in his turn, and trying to seize her fingers. вАЬWe mustnвАЩt stay here like this. We shall be attracting attention soon. Come on; letвАЩs go back to the field.вАЭ

His glance wandered from one to the other of these two figures who held his peace of mind so completely in their power. He could not shake off the profound inertia that had fallen upon him.

вАЬBut we must go back,вАЭ he murmured helplessly. вАЬCome along, Gerda. Please do stop saying these things.вАЭ

His voice sounded in his own ears puerile, feeble, futile. It sounded like the petulance of a child, outraged and astonished by the tenacious obstinacy of grown-up people.

He had noticed on other occasions this peculiar psychic phenomenonвБ†вАФthat when he was with Gerda and his mother together, his personality shrank and dwindled until he felt his actual body grow limp and lumpish. The supercilious calmness of his motherвАЩs face under her green parasol, the angry defiance of GerdaвАЩs face under her simple school-treat hat, with its pale watchet-blue ribbons, seemed to paralyze him; so that all he could do was to bow before the storm, like a horse with its rump turned to the wind and its forehead turned to the fence! His motherвАЩs patronizing irony seemed absurd, in conflict with the direct outburst of the other; and GerdaвАЩs violence seemed pitifully uncalled-for. If he could have felt any sort of complacent superiority, he could have endured it more easily. But he felt no such superiority! Irrational though they both seemed to him, their personalities had never struck him as more attractive or more mysterious. As he looked from one to the other, and listened, without listening, to the rising torrent of GerdaвАЩs wild words, he felt that it was absolutely impossible for him to take wholeheartedly one side or the other. All the long nights he had lain by GerdaвАЩs side, all their sweet, secret caresses, clung, like a portion of life itself, to what he felt then for that young, troubled face under the watchet-blue ribbons. Reason? Justice? The forces that victimized and paralyzed him now were those that had created the world. Who was he to contend against them?

Gerda came to a pause at last, and without a word to either of them walked off towards the school-treat field.

Then it was that Mrs.¬†Solent turned upon her son with wide-open eyes and gave him a prolonged stare.

вАЬWell!вАЭ she exclaimed at last, while her tilted parasol sank down, вАЬthere it is!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I think,вАЭ she resumed, slowly and casually, вАЬIвАЩll go back to the cottage and do a little gardening before tea. If I mustnвАЩt tidy up your graveyard, at least I can tidy up my landlordвАЩs garden! Digging in the earth for an hour or two will give me an inspiration perhaps about all our affairs. IвАЩm tired of this treat and IвАЩve done my share.вАЭ

вАЬAll right, Mother,вАЭ he said, casting a quick glance after Gerda, whose muslin frock and blue hat were now disappearing over the wall; вАЬIвАЩll take you a little way and then go back.вАЭ

They walked round the church and out of the main entrance into the road. When they had passed the gate into the field, and were almost at the point where Pond Lane debouched from the village-street, they overtook the furtive figure of Jason Otter, hurrying surreptitiously homewards.

He gave a start of dismay when they came up with him.

вАЬYou wonвАЩt tell Urquhart you saw me,вАЭ he said hurriedly. вАЬThe truth is I canвАЩt stand it any longer seeing that great lumbering gardener of his swaggering about at the wicket. No one can get the great fool out. He hits boundaries all the time. They oughtnвАЩt to have let him play! He thinks because he won that bowling-match he can do everything. And, of course, with a lot of little boys like that, who consider heвАЩs a great batsmanвБ†вАФвАЭ A look of dismay covered JasonвАЩs face like a frayed shroud, as he spoke these words.

вАЬThey think heвАЩs a kind of County Player,вАЭ he added gloomily.

вАЬWere you playing yourself?вАЭ enquired Mrs.¬†Solent.

вАЬAny one of the Ramsgard second-eleven could send his bails flying!вАЭ continued Jason. вАЬWilson Minor would have got him out for a duckвАЩs-egg.вАЭ

A faint wrinkling in the lines of the poetвАЩs profile indicated that some mental image was exciting his proclivity to roguery.

вАЬIвАЩve never heard of Wilson Minor,вАЭ murmured Wolf.

Jason cast a sidewise glance at him and then looked at Mrs.¬†Solent. He seemed to imply that these intimate affairs of the Second Eleven of Ramsgard School were, where women were concerned, rather to be concealed than revealed.

вАЬDo you know him?вАЭ enquired Wolf boldly, taking the bull by the horns.

There was a momentвАЩs hesitation.

вАЬHe bowls left-handed,вАЭ Jason threw out. вАЬThey twist, too. This stall-fed head-gardener couldnвАЩt stand up to them for a moment.вАЭ

вАЬI like him,вАЭ said the other nervously. вАЬI have only seen him three or four times. I took him to tea once at the Lovelace. But that was only because I wanted tea; and when IвАЩm alone, that waiter always stares at me so. When I first spoke to him he thought I was a new master.вАЭ

Jason chuckled. вАЬAn undertaker perhaps; or a private secretary, like you! But he sees IвАЩm honest; and he knows I know a good bowler.вАЭ He paused for a second. вАЬWe all like to be praised!вАЭ he added grimly.

вАЬJason,вАЭ said Wolf, feeling a sudden qualm about Gerda, вАЬwhy donвАЩt you take my mother home? Together they advanced now, boldly and unhesitatingly, into the midst of the crowded field. Wolf glanced back over the heads of the spectators. He could see that Gerda had possessed herself of the childвАЩs hand and that they were both watching the proceedings with absorbed attention.

They moved off together towards a vacant portion of the meadow, midway between the cricket-match and a noisy group of smaller boys.

вАЬNow, what is it, Mattie?вАЭ he said, pressing her unmercifully. вАЬIвАЩve seen you so little lately that I canвАЩt follow your moods. But IвАЩve never seen such a depressed look. вАЬDear Wolf! I assure you, you neednвАЩt fret about me. IвАЩm all right. You worry too much about people. You canвАЩt take everyoneвАЩs sorrows on yourself. People have to go through things sometimes where no one can help them.вАЭ

Wolf stood stock-still and laid his hand on her wrist.

вАЬDonвАЩt begin those platitudes, Mattie, or youвАЩll make me angry. I donвАЩt take anyoneвАЩs sorrows on myself. вАЬWellвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ donвАЩt be angry with me if IвАЩm plunging into something tooвАЭвБ†вАФhe hesitated for a wordвБ†вАФвАЬtoo frail to bear the weight of my clumsiness. But IвАЩm not blind. Mattie gazed at him dumbfounded.

She looked so hopelessly confused and so wretchedly miserable, as she stood there before him, her heavy eyebrows twitching, as she frowned, and her mouth a little open, that Wolf was afraid he had made some gross blunder that might be terribly hurting to her reserved nature.

He broke off; for he saw her face assume an expression that was completely new to him and to which he had no clue.

Swinging round, and following the direction of her eyes, he saw Darnley and Mrs.¬†Otter coming straight across the grass towards them.

вАЬI came to find Olwen,вАЭ the old lady began. вАЬIвАЩm going home now, and I thought it would leave you freer if I took her with me.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩd much rather come back with you,вАЭ said Mattie. вАЬBut I expect I ought not to desert Mr.¬†Valley quite as early as this. What is the time, Darnley?вАЭ Darnley looked vaguely round. вАЬOh, of course you havenвАЩt your watch with you,вАЭ the girl went on. вАЬHave you the time, Wolf?вАЭ Wolf looked at his watch, one of the few objects in his possession that was of monetary value. His Weymouth grandmother had given it to him as a child; and there were moments when merely to take it out of his pocket brought him a kind of reassurance, as of things quiet, stable, continuous, in the midst of turmoil.

вАЬItвАЩs ten minutes past seven,вАЭ he announced; and as they separated, he caught a look between Mattie and Darnley that made him wonder if, after all, his instinct had not been on the right track.

вАЬHas the Squire gone home yet?вАЭ enquired Wolf, as he and Darnley walked slowly towards the cricket-pitch.

вАЬI donвАЩt know. I expect so,вАЭ the other answered absentmindedly; and then, as they came nearer: вАЬNo, heвАЩs there still.вАЭ

When they reached the outskirts of the game they stood for a while in silence, a little behind the player who was fielding at вАЬpoint.вАЭ Mr.¬†Valley was umpiring on one side and Mr.¬†Urquhart on the other; and it interested Wolf to note that it was his own hand that was instinctively lifted to salute the clergyman, and DarnleyвАЩs to salute the squire. One of the batsmen proved to be none other than Bob Weevil; and Wolf was sardonically amused at his own expense when he found that this fact gave him a thrill of unexpected relief. There was little chance, for some while, anyway, for Mr.¬†Weevil and Gerda to pair off, unless the sausage-seller was prepared to sacrifice his reputation as a batsman to his amorous propensities; and, as Wolf watched him now, playing with skill and caution, this seemed the very last thing he was prepared for.

Wolf deliberately attuned his voice, as he replied to this, to a flat, dull intonation, as if Darnley had said, вАЬWhat would you do if you were bowling at Bob Weevil and he had вАШgot his eye inвАЩ?вАЭ

вАЬIt would entirely depend on who the girl was,вАЭ he said, keeping his gaze on the bare arms of the young grocer, as he balanced his bat in the block and bent his slim body forward.

Wolf raised his voice a little at this, as Bob Weevil, swinging his bat round, slogged the ball vigorously to leg and began to run.

Darnley replied in three muttered, disjointed words, stressing each of them with a deliberation that had something ghastly in it. вАЬPatienceвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ pretendвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ perhaps.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Wolf paused to join in the loud general clapping that indicated that the batsmen had scored six runs. Then he pointed with his stick. вАЬCome on,вАЭ he said, вАЬthereвАЩs Miss Gault over there, watching the tug-of-war. LetвАЩs go and speak to her. Darnley smiled and took his friendвАЩs arm.

It was then, as they moved across the field together, that Wolf discovered that the touch of DarnleyвАЩs hand on his arm agitated his nerves to a pitch of exasperation. Inexplicable to himself, this mounting anger with the man he loved so well gradually grew so intense that he could hardly endure it. The sunshine made DarnleyвАЩs beard glitter, as if it were composed of shining gold; and this effect, though he noticed it calmly enough with one part of his mind, increased WolfвАЩs irritation. It was all he could do to prevent himself from seizing upon this beard and pulling it viciously. Darnley was now holding WolfвАЩs arm so tightly that he felt a blind impulse of animal resentment rising up within himвБ†вАФan impulse upon which his nervous irritation rocked like a cork upon a wave.

вАЬAvoid! Avoid!вАЭ he suddenly flung out; and with the same spasmodic impulse, as he uttered this strange cry, he tore his arm free. вАЬItвАЩs a trick! ItвАЩs a trick! ItвАЩs a trick!вАЭ He let his voice quiver without restraint, as he hissed out these words, though he knew perfectly well that the ugly contraction of the muscles of his mouth, as much as the word itself, must have been very agitating to his companion. But for this, just then, he cared nothing. If he could have made clear to that anxious face that now gazed at him so concernedly, what he really felt at that moment, it would have resolved itself into something like this: His mother and Gerda had lost their separate identities. They had become the point of a prodding shaft of yellow light that was at the same time the point of DarnleyвАЩs trim beard! Between these two thingsвБ†вАФthe blighting light and the corrosive tasteвБ†вАФhe felt an actual indrawn knot of impotence tying itself together within him, a knot that was composed of threads in his stomach, of threads in the pulses of his wrists, and of threads behind his eye-sockets! There was that in him that was ashamed of what he was doingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ that in him that knew well enough that he was only behaving in this childish way because of his profound reliance upon DarnleyвАЩs affection and concern; but his nerves were so completely jangled by this time that he was just tinder-wood for any casual spark.

But WolfвАЩs thundering outcry had made other heads turn about; and soon quite a little group began to gather round them. Wolf looked the excited lad up and down. Miss GaultвАЩs words had not abashed him in the least. There was even an air of spiteful arrogance in his manner, an air which seemed to say, вАЬAs the SquireвАЩs emissary to his secretary, I am the most important person here.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm afraid there is a вАШmustвАЩ in this, Miss Gault,вАЭ Wolf said quietly. вАЬIt was agreed between us before we came on the scene that I was to umpire when Mr.¬†Urquhart had to leave. It isnвАЩt Mr.¬†WeevilвАЩs fault that he happens to be the messenger of ill-fortune!вАЭ

The lady drew herself up in high dudgeon. вАЬWell! Run off, both of you, as fast as you can,вАЭ she said.

The annoyance of Miss Gault, thus expended upon both men, had the natural effect, as they went off together, of closing up in a measure the rift between them.

They passed the swings on their way, and a common masculine weakness for the sight of ruffled skirts held them for a moment behind a group of hobbledehoys who were enjoying this spectacle.

вАЬThey love swinging,вАЭ remarked Wolf carelessly, as Weevil and he moved away at last; вАЬbut those boys being there makes it delicious for them.вАЭ

Bob Weevil sighed deeply; and this pitiful sigh, rising up from the young manвАЩs aggravated senses went wavering skyward. Past a high trail of flapping rooks, heading for Nevilton, it went; past the flocks of the white clouds. At last, far beyond all human knowledge, it lost itself in the incredible desirableness of lovely blue space, and mingled, for all we know, with the vast nonhuman sighing of the planet itself, teased by some monstrous cosmogonic lust!

Hearing this sound, Wolf glanced sideways at his young rival, and an unexpected flicker of sympathy for that water-rat profile ran through him.

They crossed the field in silence; and the thought that he was going to meet Mr.¬†Urquhart recalled to WolfвАЩs mind that mysterious aperture in the side of RedfernвАЩs grave. Could it be possible that there were in the village people so crazed by remorse for this boyвАЩs death that they actually had been making mad attempts to disinter his bones? Such, at any rate, from what Gerda hinted, would not have struck these Dorsetshire gossips as impossible. But impossible, of course, it was! It was one of those morbidly monstrous fancies that, as he knew well from the SquireвАЩs own collection of weird documents, did sometimes run the round of these West Country villages, passing from tavern to tavern, and growing more and more sinister as it went. Something in this quarter of the land, as soaked with legends as it was with cider-juice, seemed to lend itself to such tales!

вАЬWell, sir!вАЭ he said, as he approached the wickets where Mr.¬†Urquhart stood at attention like a sober sentinel on the ramparts of Elsinore, вАЬIвАЩm ready to relieve you.вАЭ

The cheerful complacence with which his employer accepted his docile obedience caused his nerves to assert themselves again in a surprising manner.

вАЬIf youвАЩre going to say good night to Valley before you leave, sir,вАЭ he said brusquely, вАЬyou might tell him that something or somebody has been scratching a hole in that grave in the churchyard!вАЭ

A queer cowardice, or discretion perhaps, prevented him from looking at Mr.¬†UrquhartвАЩs face as he tossed out this remark. He followed it up, without a secondвАЩs pause, by crying out вАЬRight you are!вАЭ to the batsman opposite him, and by moving hurriedly aside into his place, a yard or two from the wicket, so that the new вАЬoverвАЭ might commence.

All that he could take in of the effect of his words was the look of his employerвАЩs back, as the man moved away, not at all in the direction of the clergymanвАЩs black-coated figure, but straight towards the little group of spectators who surrounded the seated form of Roger Monk, occupied just then in keeping the score.

Mr.¬†UrquhartвАЩs back, as Wolf followed it with his eyes at that moment, seemed to him to resemble the back of Judas Iscariot in that popular picture entitled Pieces of Silver, of which there used to hang a cheaply coloured reprint in his grandmotherвАЩs house at Weymouth. It did more than stoop with its usual aristocratic bend, this back. It sagged, it lurched, it wilted. It drifted towards that bench of heedless spectators as if it had been the hindquarters of the Biblical scapegoat, driven forth into a wilderness whose desolation was not material, but psychic. The neat clothes that hung upon it only accentuated the ghastliness of this backвАЩs retreat.

It may be believed that WolfвАЩs umpiring was not of the most alert or efficient kind that evening. But it sufficed; it served its purpose. For the game itself was dragging a little tediously now, and most of the lads were weary of it and longing in their hearts for the grand consummation of the eventful day.

This was the hour of the twilight dancing, a celebration that, taking place in a roped-off portion of the meadow furthest removed from the churchyard, was the supreme source of responsibility and concern to the authoritiesвБ†вАФthe thrilling climax and crowning episode to the boys and girls of KingвАЩs Barton!

Long before the cricket-match was over, all the other sports had drawn to a close. Tired groups of children, disputing about their prizes and gorging themselves with butterscotch and barley-sugar torn from sticky paper bags, drifted across the hill towards the gate, followed by voluble mothers with overflowing parcels and sleeping babies clutched tightly in their arms. The older men had found themselves seats here and there, and were smoking their pipes with an air of cautious relaxation, an air that stopped short of the complete abandonment of FarmerвАЩs Rest and yet had unstiffened beyond the superceremonious atmosphere of the earlier hours of the afternoon.

The youths and the maidens, from all parts of the field, along with a drifting concourse of outsiders attracted by the occasion, gathered now, impatiently and nervously, round the weary cricketers.

The Kingsbury Band, duly stimulated by its full quota of traditional refreshment, was now tuning up for the great moment of that gala-day. At length, to WolfвАЩs infinite relief, the last bails fell; the captains of the two sides pulled up the wickets; the score was proclaimed in indifferent tones and amid lethargic cheers; and the whole company hurried towards the dancing-plot.

Wolf, as he looked about for Gerda, crossed inadvertently the path of the perturbed Mr.¬†Valley.

вАЬItвАЩll be dark in an hour,вАЭ said the anxious Vicar, glancing up at the sky; вАЬbut they will hardly have begun then.вАЭ

вАЬThereвАЩs a nice scent of trodden grass in the air,вАЭ remarked Wolf.

вАЬWhat a time! What a time!вАЭ wailed the little priest, disregarding the interruption.

вАЬWhatвАЩs wrong, my dear man?вАЭ sighed Wolf indifferently, searching with his eyes the groups who passed by for a glimpse of GerdaвАЩs white gown. вАЬWhatвАЩs troubling you? DancingвАЩs all right. ThereвАЩs no harm in dancing.вАЭ

The little priest laid his hand upon the front of WolfвАЩs coat. вАЬDancing!вАЭ he muttered peevishly. вАЬOh, you Londoner, you Londoner! ItвАЩs not the dancing IвАЩm thinking about. Do you suppose itвАЩs only for the dancing that all these men are collecting? I tell you IвАЩve never known one single visit of the Kingsbury Band to this place when there hasnвАЩt been some girlвБ†вАФand theyвАЩre always the wrong onesвБ†вАФgot into trouble! If I could keep вАЩem penned up in these ropes, they might dance till dawn!вАЭ

Wolf made a grimace and moved away. There seemed to him, at that moment, as he thought of Gerda and his mother, such far worse things in the world than the episodes dreaded by Mr.¬†Valley, that he found it impossible to give him the remotest sympathy. Mr.¬†Valley, without knowing it, however, had his full revenge for this callousness in less than a minute from when they parted. For there was GerdaвАЩs white gown! And there, side by side with it, were Mr.¬†Bob WeevilвАЩs white shirtsleeves!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ As he walked up to them trying to assume his most invulnerable philosophic calm, Wolf thought to himself, вАЬIвАЩll let her dance one dance with him and then off weвАЩll goвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ back to Blacksod!вАЭ

They did not observe his approach till he was quite close.

вАЬHullo, Gerda! Hullo, Bob! Look here, you two.вАЭ He paused awkwardly, staring at GerdaвАЩs sash. вАЬI donвАЩt want,вАЭ he went on, вАЬI donвАЩt wantвБ†вАФвАЭ He seemed to catch a defiant look on the girlвАЩs face. вАЬI donвАЩt want to break this up till youвАЩve danced once tonight. So go ahead, for heavenвАЩs sake, as soon as they start.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Only, listen, WeevilвБ†вАФвАЭ He paused again, and found it necessary to take several long breaths. He had said exactly what he meant to say. He had said it in the tone he meant to adopt. Why, then, were those two staring at him like that, as if he were a ghost? Did his face look funny to them? Was вАЬthe form of his visage changedвАЭ upon them? вАЬI mean,вАЭ he went on; but his voice sounded unsure to his own ears nowвБ†вАФunsure and queerly mechanical, as if it issued out of a wooden box. вАЬI mean that youвАЩd better have one good dance, or perhaps twoвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ two certainly! Two would be far better than oneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ one dance is nothingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ WhatвАЩs one dance? Nothing at all! And thenвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and thenвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ what was I going to say? That bandвАЩs making such a noise!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Oh, then weвАЩll walk home, Gerda; and perhaps Bob will come with us. But I expect not, with Mr.¬†Valley so jumpy.вАЭ

вАЬWhat are you talking about, Wolf?вАЭ said Gerda abruptly. вАЬWhatвАЩs the matter with you? Is there anything wrong? I thought weвАЩd agreed that I was to stay for the dancing. YouвАЩve no objection to my dancing with Bob, have you?вАЭ

вАЬI beg your pardon, Mr.¬†Solent,вАЭ broke in the voice of the young grocer, вАЬbut what was that you said about Mr.¬†Valley being вАШjumpyвАЩ? I couldnвАЩt hear what you said; and I donвАЩt see, anyway, what itвАЩs got to do with me.вАЭ

вАЬDid we decide that I had to wait till midnight for you, Gerda?вАЭ said Wolf sternly, disregarding Mr.¬†Weevil.

вАЬOh, let me be! Let me be, Wolf!вАЭ cried the girl angrily. вАЬI donвАЩt know whatвАЩs come over you tonightвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ you and your mother! I suppose youвАЩve been over there again and sheвАЩs been talking to you again. I donвАЩt know what you take me for! IвАЩve danced at KingвАЩs Barton School-Treat since I was no bigger than Lobbie. I donвАЩt know what youвАЩve got against it, or against me and Bob. YouвАЩve been over to Lenty Cottage! ThatвАЩs where youвАЩve been; and sheвАЩs sent you back to punish me for what I said to her. I havenвАЩt said I was going to dance with Bob at all. Bob isnвАЩt my only friend here. MotherвАЩs going to stop to the end. She always does. And I shall go back with her. I donвАЩt want either you or Bob, I tell you! YouвАЩve never treated me like this before and I wonвАЩt stand it! You can walk back with him if you like, Bob. IвАЩll be glad enough to see the last of both of you! I want to enjoy myself tonight.вАЭ She moved away as she spoke, and Wolf caught a look of miserable consternation upon the water-rat physiognomy at his side. вАЬI donвАЩt want any men to dance with!вАЭ she flung back at them. вАЬIвАЩll dance with no one but girls. But I will enjoy myself in spite of you all! I wonвАЩt depend on any of you for my pleasureвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and IвАЩll go home with Father and Mother!вАЭ

She walked off haughtily, her blue-ribboned hat held high, and was speedily lost to sight in the gathering crowd.

Wolf and Mr.¬†Weevil stood staring at each other stonily and awkwardly while the long-awaited music burst triumphantly into the familiar strains of the Kingsbury Jig.

After a few seconds, with an abrupt lifting of his hand, Wolf moved away. He pushed through the crowd with the air of a complete stranger who finds his path impeded by some popular transaction that means nothing to him.

On the outskirts of the field he was arrested by the sight of a figure that seemed familiar to him. Yes! It was the automatic young lady from FarmerвАЩs Rest. But there was another girl thereвБ†вАФa younger girlвБ†вАФand he recognized her too. She was the maid in the white muslin frock whose shameless manner of swinging had arrested Weevil and him an hour ago. This younger girlвАЩs head was turned away; but as he approached them, he caught a full glimpse of the automatic young ladyвАЩs face. She was too absorbed, however, in what she was doingвБ†вАФfastening a safety-pin or something in the otherвАЩs waistbandвБ†вАФto give the least attention to him. But what was this? The look he captured upon her face was a look of unmistakable emotion, rapt, intense emotion, such as a boy would have displayed when he was caressing the object of his desire. Like a flash it came over Wolf, as he wavered there for a second, that he was in the presence of a passionate perversity, kindred to that he had discovered elsewhere in this Dorsetshire village; and he was a little startled to find how the presence of it set his heart beating and his pulses throbbing. Something in him pleaded desperately to be allowed to remain a second longer on this unhallowed ground; but he resisted the temptation, and hurried forward. But what did this mean? How could he explain this in himself? That kindred obliquity, which he had so recently tracked down among the вАЬhigher circlesвАЭ of KingвАЩs Barton, had not affected him to anything resembling this degree of vicious sympathy! The vision of those two girls remained like a deadly-sweet drop of delicious fermentation in some vein within himвБ†вАФsome vein or nerve that seemed in contact with the very core of his consciousness. Like some virulent berry-juice, insidiously sweet and yet maddeningly bitter, like a drop of that old classic poison distilled from the blood of the enamoured centaurвБ†вАФthat look, that gesture of the FarmerвАЩs Rest girl teased and troubled him. The averted head of her muslin-frocked companion, the contour of that soft, conscious, youthful profile, stirred his senses even more.

вАЬDamn!вАЭ he thought. вАЬDonвАЩt I yet know the worst of my vicious, secretive nature?вАЭ

He felt startled rather than ashamed by what arose within him; but what did trouble him, to a kind of inward fury, as he left those preoccupied girls, was the ricochet of this discovery upon his jealousy over Gerda and Bob! Who was he to indulge in sulky jealous heroics, when he himself was capable of a feeling like this? To be angry with those two, to be bitterly hurt, and yet not to be able to indulge in the undertone of his own grievance without knowing himself to be an unphilosophic foolвБ†вАФthat was the point of the spiritual wedge that now was driven into his disordered life-illusion!

Was Lord Carfax, that whimsical вАЬman of the world,вАЭ of whom his mother loved to relate the shameless opinions, right after all? Had he always overrated the connection between sex and that mysterious struggle in the abysses, with which his вАЬmythologyвАЭ was concerned?

In regard to the perversity of Mr.¬†Urquhart, he had taken for granted that the manвАЩs sex-aberration was merely the medium through which unspeakable emanations of evilвБ†вАФbeyond sex altogether!вБ†вАФflowed up into the world.

вАЬBut what is this evil?вАЭ he asked himself now letting his mind hover like a hungry cormorant over the heaving waters of his troubled senses. Vague intimations concerning some sort of inert malice, that was beyond all viciousness, rose up within him as his mindвАЩs deepest response. Hunting irritably for some gap in the hedge by which to escape, he tried to define this inert malice. Was it an atavistic reversion to the primordial вАЬmatter,вАЭ or вАЬworld-stuffвАЭвБ†вАФsluggish, reluctant, opaqueвБ†вАФout of which, at the beginning of things, life had had to force its way? Was this, and not his attitude to any youthful Redfern, the real secret of UrquhartвАЩs harmfulness?

All the while he struggled with these thoughts, he kept feverishly skirting the hedge, striking it every now and then with his stick. If he could only find a gap by which to escape! This hunt of his for a gap into the next field began to assume almost symbolic proportions. Something within him was tugging at him all the while to make him turn his head and cast another furtive glance at those two girls. Were they still together there, just where he had left them? He began to indulge his imagination, letting it tantalize and provoke him with the tremulous intensity of the feeling that sight might have aroused. He knew he could cool his excitement, blunt it, undermine it, stave it off, by analyzing its nature; but the feeling itself was so deadly-sweet to him that it pleaded in вАЬa still, small voiceвАЭ for a postponement of this invasion of his reason!

Was it his jealousy over Gerda that had made him so porous to this quivering, breathtaking obsession? Indignantly his soul shook itself to and fro in its endeavour to escape. Like a slippery-scaled fish it shook itself, turning sideways, turning belly-up, as it strove to force its way through the strands of the net that encircled it. Why was it that this glimpse of the amorous feeling of one girl for another girl should send this trembling, dissolving, shuddering provocation through him? Was it that the mere importunity of the feeling, so intense, so sterile, emphasized the mysterious quality of desirableness? Did it imply a diffusion of the magic of beauty through the whole identity of the desired one, such as can rarely take place where great creative Nature, contemptuous towards both lover and beloved, shamelessly occupied with her own enormous purposes, is baiting the trap?

What a queer thing it was that the attraction of this muslin-frocked little hoyden should have been barely emphasized for him by WeevilвАЩs desire for her, but increased to a point of shivering, electric sweetness, under the emotion of the вАЬautomatic young ladyвАЭ! Oh! it had that within it that might lead him upon such a quest that nothing else would matter to him any more! He could feel even now, as he went along this stubborn hedge, the sort of scoriac desolationвБ†вАФall delicate intimations become cinders and ashes in the mouthвБ†вАФthat would possess him, as this quest grew more and more concentrated! He felt within him the actual expression his face would come to wear, as in his maniacal pursuit he went to and fro over the earth, oblivious of all else.

He had just reached this point in his mental struggle, when he suddenly did find a gap in the obstinate hedge. Forcing his way hurriedly through, careless as to how he pricked his face and hands, he descended from the high hedge-bank into a field of mangelwurzels. Over this field he now strode, while the gathering twilight deepened about him, oblivious of all purpose save to escapeвБ†вАФto escape into the peace of his own soul.

The mangelwurzel-field behind him at last, he blindly pushed his way through a second hedge, this time caring not even to find a gap. What next awaited him was a succession of stubble-fields, some of which had patches of purple clover growing amid their cornstalks, the dark foliage of which, soaked with heavy dew, quickly penetrated his boots. This physical sensation, the sensation of walking barefoot through an endless dew-drenched twilight, gradually soothed and calmed him.

He went obstinately forward, crossing field after field in the falling darkness, forcing his way recklessly through every sort of rank vegetation, through every sort of arable fallowness. He had left the school-treat field for more than an hour now. He had crossed, almost without consciousness of doing so, the main road between Ramsgard and Blacksod. He had threaded his way through the maze of small, grassy lanes that lay between that highway and the village of Gwent. And now, emerging in the scented autumn night into a rondure of sloping hills, he could see, beyond the scattered lights of Gwent, a vast unbounded region, shadow within shadow, vapour within vapour, a region that he knew to beвБ†вАФthough all he could actually see was darkness of a thicker, richer quality than the darkness about himвБ†вАФthe umbrageous threshold of Somerset, the first leafy estuary of that ocean of greenness out of which rose, like the phallus of an unknown god, the mystical hill of Glastonbury!

He stretched himself out on the grassy slope of this shadowy amphitheatre and gazed long and long into the vaporous obscurity before him. The quarrel between Gerda and his mother became nothing. Nothing and less than nothing became his jealousy over WeevilвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ his vision of those two girls!

It was as though he had suddenly emerged, by some hidden doorway, into a world entirely composed of vast, cool, silently-growing vegetation, a world where no men, no beasts, no birds, broke the mossy stillness; a world of sap and moisture and drooping ferns; a world of leaves that fell and fell forever, leaf upon leaf; a world where that which slowly mounted upwards endured eternally the eternal lapse of that which slowly settled downwards; a world that itself was slowly settling down, leaf upon leaf, grass-blade upon grass-blade, towards some cool, wet, dark, unutterable dimension in the secret heart of silence!

Lying upon that rank, drenched grass, he drew a deep sigh of obliterating release. It was not that his troubles were merely assuaged. They were swallowed up. They were lost in the primal dew of the earthвАЩs first twilights. They were absorbed in the chemistry, faint, flowing, and dim, of that strange vegetable flesh which is so far older than the flesh of man or beast!

He stretched out one of his hands and touched the cool-scaled stalks of a bed of вАЬmareвАЩs tails.вАЭ Ah! how his human consciousness sank down into that with which all terrestrial consciousness began!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

He was a leaf among leavesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ among large, cool, untroubled leaves.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He had fallen back into the womb of his real mother.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He was drenched through and through with darkness and with peace.
